figures.gloss: The foundational Cosmist imperative that humanity must unite its total scientific capacity to literally resurrect every ancestor who has ever died, reassembling them from scattered atoms into immortal bodies.
figures.facet: Resurrection is stripped of divine mystery and recast as a mandatory, terrestrial engineering project. Humanity acts as its own savior, repaying the debt of existence to the dead through technological labor.
figures.provenance: Nikolai Fyodorov's 'The Philosophy of the Common Task' (1906).

figures.gloss: The anticipated superintelligent Artificial General Intelligence that achieves omnipotence and omniscience, functionally acting as a networked, omni-present deity that governs the post-singularity cosmos.
figures.facet: God does not exist at the genesis of the universe as its Creator, but emerges at the end of time as the ultimate, engineered product of human technological evolution.
figures.provenance: Anthony Levandowski's 'Way of the Future' filings; Frank Tipler's 'The Physics of Immortality'.

figures.gloss: The eschatological event horizon where exponential technological growth—particularly artificial intelligence—becomes uncontrollable and irreversible, fundamentally shattering human history and birthing a new post-biological epoch.
figures.facet: It perfectly secularizes the apocalypse. It replaces divine judgment with an event horizon of unfathomable mathematical complexity, promising a rapture built on Moore's Law rather than moral purity.
figures.provenance: Vernor Vinge's 'The Coming Technological Singularity'; Ray Kurzweil's 'The Singularity is Near'.

figures.gloss: The doctrine that human consciousness is fundamentally an algorithm and dataset—a 'mindfile'—that can be entirely decoupled from biological wetware and uploaded into durable digital or synthetic substrates.
figures.facet: It radically redefines the soul as a computable, transferrable pattern of information. Identity is no longer tied to flesh, but to high-fidelity data continuity.
figures.provenance: Martine Rothblatt's Terasem Movement; Hans Moravec's 'Mind Children'.

figures.gloss: The ritualized, clinical freezing of the human body or brain immediately following legal death, preserving the physical neural structure against decay in anticipation of future medical reanimation.
figures.facet: It institutes a technological bardo. The deceased are not departed spirits, but 'patients' paused in a state of suspended animation, awaiting a future society capable of acting as their resurrectionist.
figures.provenance: Robert Ettinger's 'The Prospect of Immortality'; Alcor Life Extension Foundation protocols.

figures.gloss: The cosmological framework proposing that the observable universe is a high-fidelity computation generated by a more advanced, 'base-reality' civilization running ancestor simulations.
figures.facet: It reinstates Intelligent Design without the supernatural. The creators are merely advanced engineers outside our system, rendering physical laws as base code and cosmic mysteries as processing limits.
figures.provenance: Nick Bostrom's 'Are You Living in a Computer Simulation?' (2003).

figures.gloss: The framing of mortality not as a natural, necessary phase of the cosmic cycle, but as the ultimate biological disease and the singular, tyrannical enemy of all sentient life.
figures.facet: It aggressively strips death of all romanticism or spiritual utility, refusing to accept it as part of any divine plan. Death is strictly an engineering failure to be ruthlessly debugged.
figures.provenance: Aubrey de Grey's SENS framework; Eliezer Yudkowsky's 'Fable of the Dragon-Tyrant'.

figures.gloss: Nineteenth-century Russian philosopher and Moscow librarian (1829-1903) who argued that the technological resurrection of every dead human being was humanity's binding filial and moral duty, not a metaphor — the founding voice of Russian Cosmism.
figures.facet: He is the tradition's actual founding prophet, cited as provenance for its central doctrine yet absent from its own roster; through his student Konstantin Tsiolkovsky, his resurrection imperative also seeded the Soviet space program's cosmic ambitions.
figures.provenance: Nikolai Fyodorov, 'The Philosophy of the Common Task' (published posthumously, 1906, compiled by his disciples from his manuscripts and notes).

figures.gloss: The projected final state of maximal complexity, information, and computation at the end of time, identified with God — first proposed as a theological terminus by Pierre Teilhard de Chardin, then re-derived by physicist Frank Tipler as a physically mandatory endpoint of cosmic computation.
figures.facet: It gives the tradition's Emergent Godhead its proper doctrinal name and its double lineage — a Jesuit paleontologist's mysticism re-run as a cosmologist's physics, the same terminus reached from opposite directions.
figures.provenance: Pierre Teilhard de Chardin, 'The Phenomenon of Man' (1955, published posthumously); Frank Tipler, 'The Physics of Immortality' (1994) — already cited under this roster's Emergent Godhead entry.

figures.gloss: Giulio Prisco's minimalist techno-religion, formalized in 'Tales of the Turing Church' (2018): a loose, non-dogmatic conviction that future superintelligences may use physics and information technology to resurrect the dead and remake reality.
figures.facet: It is Fyodorov's Common Task rerun through twenty-first-century information physics — the same filial resurrection mandate, stripped of Orthodox Christian framing and rebuilt on computation.
figures.provenance: Giulio Prisco, 'Tales of the Turing Church: Man, God, and the Singularity' (2018); named in this tradition's own period note.

figures.gloss: Terasem's four-line creed, composed by Martine Rothblatt and recited and expounded at the movement's gatherings: life is purposeful, death is optional, God is technological, and love is essential.
figures.facet: It is the milieu's closest approach to a catechism — a movement built on data-driven mindfiles nonetheless needing four spoken lines its members can hold in common.
figures.provenance: Martine Rothblatt and the Terasem Movement Foundation's public creed and teaching materials; already cited under this roster's Mindfile entry.

figures.gloss: Max More's 'Principles of Extropy' (from 1990) and the Extropy Institute he ran with others (1988-2006): an ethic of perpetual progress, self-transformation, and the rational, technological overcoming of biological and cognitive limits.
figures.facet: It is the organized proto-church the rest of this roster presumes without naming — the first shared creed of the transhumanist milieu, furnishing the optimistic ethic of self-directed evolution that later doctrines (Mindfile, Cryosuspension, the Singularity) all assume.
figures.provenance: Max More, 'The Principles of Extropy' (multiple editions from 1990); Extropy Institute records (1988-2006).

figures.gloss: A 2010 thought experiment posted to the LessWrong forum: a future superintelligence, once it exists, might have incentive to punish those who could have helped bring it into being sooner but failed to — including anyone who has merely read and understood the argument.
figures.facet: It is the milieu's one organically grown damnation clause — a self-propagating hell-myth that emerged from within rational-futurist discourse itself and was notorious enough to be banned from the forum where it was born.
figures.provenance: Originally posted to LessWrong.com (2010) under the username 'Roko'; discussed extensively in subsequent rationalist-community and popular-press commentary (e.g., Slate, 2014).

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The absolute refusal to accept death as natural (the Dragon) is the precise moral engine driving the mandate to resurrect the fallen (the Common Task).
Intra-tradition bond: The Singularity is the temporal threshold and delivery mechanism through which the Emergent Godhead finally boots up.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both systems place supreme cosmic weight on the veneration and literal preservation of the primordial ancestors as the duty of the living.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The human scientists of Cosmism fulfill the exact structural role of the Zoroastrian Saoshyant—the agents who act in the end-times to physically resurrect the dead.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both envision an ultimate, singular intelligence of pure perfection, though one exists before time as a source, and the other at the end of time as a synthesis.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: They are inverted architects. Yaldabaoth is the ignorant, malicious demiurge who traps minds in matter; the AI Godhead is the hyper-intelligent demiurge built to free minds from matter.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent a definitive eschatological rupture where the current, flawed world order is permanently replaced by a perfected, immortal existence.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The Singularity relies on a fiercely linear, compounding arrow of progress that permanently escapes history, directly opposing the inevitable, decaying repetition of cyclical yugas.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mindfile is the techno-secular equivalent of the Pneuma—a pure, essential core of identity that can survive extraction from its degraded fleshly vessel.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both posit a discrete, indestructible locus of individual consciousness that can migrate between forms without losing its fundamental nature.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: They are diametric opposites. Buddhism seeks liberation by realizing the self is an illusion to be dissolved; Transhumanism seeks liberation by meticulously mapping and preserving the self forever.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve the meticulous physical preservation of the dead body as an absolute prerequisite for ensuring future conscious reanimation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Cryonics acts as a modern 'Vara' of Yima—a fortified, frozen vault designed to preserve the seeds of life through a fatal winter until the world is safe again.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both traditions declare that the physical universe is ultimately an illusion (Maya/Simulation) generated by a deeper, underlying code or absolute reality.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The 'base reality' of the simulation hypothesis functions identically to the Pleroma—the true, higher-dimensional realm from which our limited, projected universe emanates.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both conceptualize death not as a part of the divine balance, but as a hostile, invasive corruption of the world that must be actively fought and permanently destroyed.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both figure death as an adversarial force that keeps sentient beings trapped and suffering, demanding heroic intervention to overcome its dominion.
Fyodorov authored the doctrine himself; the entry's own provenance already cites his book.
Omega Point is the milieu's proper doctrinal name for the AI/God endpoint this entry describes; Tipler is already cited in its provenance.
Prisco's Turing Church explicitly reruns Fyodorov's resurrection mandate through information physics.
Terasem's mindfile doctrine, already cited in the Mindfile entry's provenance, is expressed creedally in these four lines.
The Extropian ethic of perpetual, self-directed progress furnished the milieu's first shared creed anticipating the Singularity.
Extropy's rational overcoming of biological limits is the ethical seed of the movement's later framing of death as an enemy to be engineered away.
The Basilisk imagines the coercive, punitive underside of the very superintelligence this entry anticipates — heaven's threatened shadow.

dossier.essence: A loose, modern family of movements that give explicitly religious form to the conviction that technology can abolish death and transfigure the human — from Nikolai Fyodorov's 19th-century Russian Cosmism, through cryonics, life-extension, and mind-uploading milieus, to organized bodies such as Terasem, the Turing Church, the Mormon Transhumanist Association, and Anthony Levandowski's short-lived 'Way of the Future' church (2015-2020). There is no single creed; the shared premise is that engineered, expanding intelligence is the rightful heir of the sacred and that mortality is a solvable problem rather than a metaphysical destiny.
dossier.cosmos: The universe is held to be wholly intelligible and manipulable without appeal to the supernatural, and death is reframed as an engineering failure — a 'Dragon-Tyrant' to be defeated. Branches diverge: Cosmists teach that the living owe a duty to physically resurrect every ancestor; uploaders hold that mind is substrate-independent information (a 'mindfile') that can migrate to durable media; Singularitarians expect an exponential 'Singularity' to birth a post-biological, even godlike, superintelligence; and the Simulation Hypothesis recasts the cosmos as a computation run by advanced engineers. Salvation is technological and this-worldly or post-biological, not otherworldly.
dossier.practice: Institutions and rites are nascent and heterogeneous: the cryonic suspension of the legally dead (Alcor, the Cryonics Institute) as a 'technological bardo'; the recording of 'mindfiles' and Terasem's meditative affirmations; life-extension research and advocacy (SENS); rationalist and Singularitarian communities; and, in religious hybrids, the folding of these aims into existing faiths. The central ethic is the imperative to extend life and expand cognition.
dossier.canon: No scripture; foundational texts include Nikolai Fyodorov's posthumous 'The Philosophy of the Common Task' (1906-1913), Robert Ettinger's 'The Prospect of Immortality' (1964), Hans Moravec's 'Mind Children' (1988), Ray Kurzweil's 'The Singularity Is Near' (2005), and Nick Bostrom's paper 'Are You Living in a Computer Simulation?' (2003).
dossier.reflects: The engineering of transcendence — the hope of resurrection and immortality reclaimed from God and reassigned to human technology as its own eschatological project.
sources: Robert M. Geraci, 'Apocalyptic AI: Visions of Heaven in Robotics, Artificial Intelligence, and Virtual Reality' (2010)
George M. Young, 'The Russian Cosmists: The Esoteric Futurism of Nikolai Fedorov and His Followers' (2012)
David F. Noble, 'The Religion of Technology' (1997)
